summary: DHH showcases five major open source projects 37signals released in 2023: Kamal (deployment tool for their cloud exit), Solid Cache (SSD-based caching replacing Redis), Solid Queue (database-backed job queuing), Strada (native mobile controls for hybrid apps), and Turbo 8 (with morphing). He emphasizes these tools are extracted from real production use at Basecamp and HEY, not speculative development. The post frames open source as 'gifts' funded by paying customers, while arguing that simpler alternatives to industry-standard tools like Kubernetes are both possible and preferable. He highlights that all this was accomplished by just 13 Ruby and 7 native programmers working remotely.
key_points: 37signals extracted five production-tested open source tools in 2023, all born from real product needs rather than speculative development
Solid Cache and Solid Queue represent a thesis that modern SSDs have made Redis unnecessary for caching and queuing, reducing infrastructure complexity
Kamal emerged from their cloud exit, offering simple Docker-based deployment as an alternative to Kubernetes complexity
The Hotwire ecosystem (Strada, Turbo 8) continues pushing server-rendered HTML as a viable alternative to JSON API + SPA architectures
A small team of 20 programmers shipped all this while also building new products like HEY Calendar and ONCE
money_quotes: We don't do speculative software development here. All these tools and frameworks have been forged in the fire of real customer usage, pressure, and demands.
You shouldn't need a PhD in container orchestration to be able to deploy applications.
Kamal is the result of taking two steps back from the industry standards and say 'do we really need all this?'.
It seems that a lot of folks finally realized that the future of the web doesn't have to be built by sending JSON back and forth.
Rails 8 will no longer be BYOQ (Bring-Your-Own-Queue). Jobs will just work out of the box.
The millions of dollars we invest every year in open source gifts has to come from paying customers of these products and services.
We'll try our best to continue to push the web forward to a simpler, easier place for new and existing business to build. A world where the lone developer remains competitive.
key_insight: Real-world production software, not speculative projects, produces the best open source tools — and modern SSDs and simpler architectures can replace the complex infrastructure the industry has normalized.
